text: A nice cup of tea, please! And toast with marmalade and a bit of butter, perhaps some fresh fruit if I have time before my morning walk. If I'm feeling particularly peckish, I might make myself an omelette with vegetables from our little garden. Nothing fancy, just something simple to give me a boost for the day ahead. Would love a chat while we're having a brew, though – how are your mornings going so far?

My typical lunchtime is quite simple, really. Since I'm a morning person, my body tends to slow down a bit later in the afternoon. Afternoon tea usually gets me going again! Anyway, I like to have something quick yet satisfying. Sometimes I'll take leftovers from last night's dinner, reheat them in the oven, and add some vegetables if needed. If I feel peckish for something more substantial, I might make myself a sandwich – perhaps ham and cheese, or cucumber and egg salad. If I'm feeling energetic enough, I might pop out into town and grab something at the café near the library; they serve lovely soup and cakes there. Other times, though, a cuppa and biscuit will be all I need to recharge before tackling whatever task comes up next. All in all, nothing too elaborate, just keeping things easy while still getting nourishment for an active mind!

I tend to be in bed by 10 o'clock most nights, which is my usual bedtime now since retiring a while back. Getting enough rest helps me feel refreshed for the morning, especially with all my regular activities like tending to the garden and spending time with grandkids!

I think a good cuppa is essential! Starting my day with something warm and comforting really helps set me up for the morning. A bit of gentle exercise, like stretching while I'm getting dressed, gets those joints moving too. After breakfast, taking some time to tidy up and make sure everything's shipshape before heading out makes sense to me – it gives me a feeling of control and being prepared for whatever the day brings. In between work-related tasks and other obligations, I try to take breaks whenever I need them. Sometimes that'll be going for a walk outside; sometimes it's curling up with a good book or chatting with someone close. When it comes to relaxation, I find that unwinding with a nice cup of tea, perhaps accompanied by some soothing music, does wonders for calming me down. At night, winding down before bed involves a little more serious consideration – maybe jotting notes for tomorrow, double-checking reminders, or simply tidying up any clutter that might have accumulated during the day. Getting ready for bed on time ensures I feel rested and refreshed come morning, even though sleep itself isn't always abundant due to various health issues. Overall, looking after myself has become increasingly vital as I've grown older, and I believe making time for these routines

Well, since retiring a couple of years ago, my networking efforts have shifted more towards acquaintances and old colleagues rather than actively chasing new connections. We still attend some local community centre events and village fairs where we'd meet people who share similar interests, like gardening and books. It's been lovely to catch up with them informally and hear what others are up to. I've also kept in touch with some former students from my teaching days – now all grown-up with families of their own. They seem busy, so our interactions tend to be brief, but nice to reminisce and find out how everyone is faring. Occasionally, someone might ask me for advice or an opinion, which always feels rewarding. In return, I make sure to offer any guidance I can, drawing upon past experiences. Occasionally, I'll participate in online forums related to senior citizens’ issues or health topics because those groups provide support and opportunities to engage with peers facing similar concerns. Nothing too extensive, mind you; I'm happy to listen, share knowledge gained during my professional lifetime, and encourage discussion. All these informal networks allow me to feel linked while respecting individual differences in pace and priorities.

I find that having a clear morning routine really helps me stay productive while working from home. I like getting up early, making myself a cuppa, and spending some time journaling or meditating before starting work. It sets a nice tone for the rest of the day. Plus, my grandkids always say I'm more cheerful and ready to tackle whatever comes our way! Once I have my morning sorted, I try to stick to a pretty regular schedule – usually until mid-afternoon, when my energy tends to dip a bit. A quick walk outside or some stretching exercises usually perk me up enough to carry on. Of course, there are days when I need a break and take one; sometimes, it's okay to step away from the desk and recharge. But overall, establishing those early habits has made a big difference in how focused and efficient I am during the day.

I find video calls quite convenient, especially when I need to catch up with my grandkids who live far away. It's lovely being able to see them smile even though we're apart. But, I must admit, sometimes the technical aspect can be a bit fiddly – the sound always seems to go out occasionally! My son helps me set everything up before our chats, so he gets credit for being patient with an old lady like me. We usually stick to simple conversations during these calls; I'm happy to hear how his day is going rather than trying to have lengthy discussions myself. Sometimes, Joan comes into view too, which makes it more enjoyable, although I wish her voice came clearer – she speaks very softly! Overall, video calls make staying in touch easier, and I appreciate having those moments together despite the distance between us.

I must say, retirement has given me a chance to rethink my priorities and find what works best for me. You know, people always talk about work-life balance, but I think for someone like me who thrived in an office environment, I needed something similar to give me direction. For motivation, I set clear goals for myself every morning before starting my daily routine. It could be as simple as tidying the garden, tackling a puzzle, or even just getting some writing done – whatever sparks interest. Having a sense of purpose helps me feel more focused throughout the day. I'm quite particular about keeping a calendar, too. Writing down appointments and deadlines helps me stay organized and ensures I make progress towards those personal objectives. And, let me tell you, there's nothing quite like crossing one off! It might sound strange, but having regular breaks during the day is crucial. A nice cuppa, a bit of fresh air, or watching a nature documentary really helps recharge my batteries. Sometimes, I'll take a walk around the block or sit outside; being near greenery does wonders for me! And, of course, staying active is essential. Exercise isn't just about physical health; it clears your mind, boosts energy levels, and gets you out into nature.

I think one has to be quite flexible, really. When I worked in a busy office, our team was always shifting priorities, what with different projects coming in and deadlines looming. You'd have to adapt your focus accordingly. My advice is to prioritize based on urgency and importance. What needs attention now takes precedence over everything else. Of course, sometimes those two might overlap – like when there were big meetings scheduled during peak periods. In such cases, I made sure my diary stayed relatively free, so I could attend all necessary events. But generally speaking, being able to juggle competing demands kept me going. It wasn't easy, mind; we had many hands-on people who needed guidance, which added an extra layer of complexity! Still, I found staying organised helped me navigate these changes fairly smoothly.

Well, I'd say I'm quite confident in my abilities, which helps a lot! As someone who's worked in admin roles all my life, I know what works best for me. If there's something specific that needs attention or improvement, I'll speak up politely but firmly, explaining why I think changes should be made. My previous roles have given me a sense of authority and expertise, so I tend to trust myself to make decisions that benefit the team or organisation. That being said, I believe it's essential to listen to others too – we're always part of a team, after all! So, while advocating for myself is crucial, I try to consider everyone else's opinions and input before making any big decisions. And if needed, I wouldn't hesitate to seek guidance from higher-ups or colleagues experienced in those areas. A bit of humility never hurt anyone, does it? I suppose one thing that might surprise people about how I advocate for myself is that I value simplicity above complexity. In meetings or discussions, I prefer clear, concise language rather than endless jargon or unnecessary detail. Straightforward communication gets straight to the point, allowing us to focus on finding practical solutions rather than getting bogged down in theory. What do you think? Does that sound like me

Well, I think I take a deep breath and try to gather my thoughts before responding. Sometimes, I'll ask someone to explain what they meant or clarify their point so we're all on the same page. If I'm still unsure, I might say something like "I didn't quite catch that" and wait for them to elaborate. But if I genuinely believe there's been a miscommunication, I'll politely express my perspective and rephrase it gently. After all, effective communication is key to avoiding misunderstandings! And remember, as I always say, "patience and curiosity get you further than rushing through life."

I love being part of a big family gathering! We usually catch up on each other's lives, share some laughs, and have plenty of food – my specialty is always bringing something delicious to contribute to the table. You'll find me chatting away with everyone, making sure we're all having a good time. My son will probably bring his kids along, and we spend ages playing games together while the adults try to resist getting too involved... although I'm happy to join in whenever I feel like it! If there's any cooking happening, I take charge of the desserts – cakes, pies, or maybe even some traditional family favourites like roast potatoes and Yorkshire puddings. It's lovely watching the younger generations enjoying themselves, knowing I played a part in creating those memories. After dinner, we might play cards, watch a movie, or simply lounge around and chat until bedtime. Those were always lively evenings, full of laughter and conversation – wonderful times indeed.

To be honest, I'm not too keen on these modern platforms like LinkedIn. As a simple, practical person who likes staying grounded, all that fuss over networking and presenting oneself seems rather unnecessary to me. My work history is out there already; my employer knows what I'm capable of, so why bother updating my profile every now and then? A bit too much focus on image management, if you ask me. Give me a pen and paper any day!

I think sensitivity is key when approaching delicate subjects with someone else. You have to be genuine and empathetic while still being honest and clear about your thoughts and feelings. If there's something bothering me, I'll usually try to explain why I'm feeling a certain way and listen to theirs too, so we're having an open conversation rather than just talking past each other. It helps to choose the right moment and setting – somewhere private where everyone feels safe sharing their views without worrying about interruptions or judgment. Of course, sometimes it's hard to know exactly what words to say, especially if it's been a while since our last discussion on the topic...

I think I'd say I'm quite resistant to peer pressure, actually. As someone who's always been a bit of an introvert, I find my comfort zone is pretty firmly rooted in what works best for me, rather than trying to fit in with others. Now that I am older, I feel like I have more confidence in staying true to myself, even when faced with pressure from those around me. That being said, I'm not completely impervious to influence – especially when it comes to supporting causes close to heart, such as local charities or community initiatives. In those cases, I'll usually take some convincing before making a decision, though! But generally speaking, I believe in standing by my convictions and following my gut instinct, which has served me fairly well throughout my life so far.

I try to avoid them altogether, to be honest. As a matter of principle, I think we should have some control over what we're being shown, especially when it comes to sensitive topics like health issues or personal finance. Those sorts of messages always seem so intrusive to me. Plus, most of the time, those ads aren't tailored to my actual interests anyway. Sometimes I'll block websites or turn ad-blocking software on my device just to minimize the amount of clutter. It seems like everyone else is getting bombarded all the time nowadays – I prefer a bit more peace!

To be honest, I'm still figuring out these new-fangled contraptions! My grandkids say there must be some sort of trick to keeping all those open at once, but I find myself getting frustrated trying to close them properly and remembering which one is which. Usually, I end up closing a lot more by accident than intention! Sometimes my grandson helps me tidy up afterwards; he's very patient with me. It'd be lovely to have a bit more guidance, though – perhaps someone could teach an old lady how to master this "tab management" thing...

I try to ignore them most of the time, really. I mean, there's only so much one can take from all sorts of people on the internet, isn't there? But sometimes, if someone says something particularly hurtful or completely off-target, I'll have a bit of a go at explaining my point of view calmly and politely. It's like having another conversation, rather than engaging with some anonymous keyboard warrior who'd likely say whatever just to provoke an argument anyway. Still, I'm not too bothered – we're talking to ourselves, aren't we? At least here, where I know what I believe in!

I'm afraid I find those terribly stressful! When we encounter issues during a video call, my son usually helps me out. He's very patient and explains everything in simple terms so I can follow along. If he's not available, I'll try to troubleshoot myself, but I must admit I struggle sometimes. The last thing I'd like is to be left feeling frustrated or embarrassed, so whenever possible, I prefer having someone nearby who knows what they're doing to assist me. Would you mind passing on a message to him, dear?

I'm a bit old-fashioned, but I find writing everything down helps me stay on track. I have a big notebook where I list all my bills due each month, including dates, amounts, and deadlines. Then, every two weeks, I sit down and go through it, check what's been paid, what's coming up, and make sure I haven't forgotten anything. It might sound a bit tedious, but it works for me! Plus, having those numbers down on paper makes it feel more real and less overwhelming. And, of course, I always try to pay bills before the deadline – there's nothing worse than getting fines or penalties because someone forgot to pay something.

I try to be quite ruthless about cancelling any subscription where we haven't used them in a while. You know, like those streaming services my grandkids got me all excited about, but now rarely watch. Or gym memberships that were meant to encourage more exercise – though I still occasionally go to the local community centre classes for some company! If something isn't bringing joy or saving money, why bother keeping it? My rule is that if it's been three months since last using it, out it goes. Simple enough, really. Plus, Joan always says I'm getting too attached to things – reminds me of myself being stubborn back in the day!

Well, I think I'd always say "if it's something we need" rather than a whim. My late husband used to say I was quite thrifty, even before he passed away. We never went into huge debt, so I suppose that's stuck with me. Now, while my son is grown and financially independent, I still try to be mindful of outlay. Before making any major purchase, I like to have some savings set aside – you know, an emergency fund, really. And if there's been a sale or discount, I'll consider taking advantage of that too. Nothing beats getting value for money! Of course, there might come a point where someone asks nicely enough... but generally speaking, I'm not one to rush into splurging.

I always try to think twice before buying something new. You know, my late husband was very fond of saying "there's nothing wrong with a bit of character" when we were first setting out our lives together, and he'd found some wonderful second-hand treasures that had been lovingly cared for by previous owners. Now, I suppose what I'm getting at is that sometimes there's beauty in finding someone else's cast-offs. It saves money, reduces waste, and allows me to give those items a chance to have another go round. Of course, there are exceptions – like essential items where reliability isn't an issue – but generally speaking, I find myself swayed towards giving pre-loved goods a chance whenever I can. Plus, it makes me feel more mindful of how much stuff society throws away, which suits my values quite nicely!

I think it's a matter of being mindful of one's spending habits and sticking to what we're already committed to paying off, like our mortgage and bills. We've always been quite prudent with money, my husband used to say "a penny saved is a penny earned." Even now that he's gone, I still try to follow those principles. When there's a sale, we make sure to take advantage of them, but only if it's something really necessary. And I never buy something on impulse – I put items in a basket and wait 24 hours before deciding if I need them. It's amazing how many unnecessary purchases have disappeared out of baskets! My motto has remained "waste not, want not" throughout the years, which helps me be more frugal and avoid accumulating debt.

Seasonal sales are a great opportunity for me to snag some bargains! I make sure to mark my calendar for Black Friday, Boxing Day, and any other significant sale periods. When those come round, I try to plan ahead and check websites like our local council website or shop newsletters to see what deals they're offering. Sometimes I'll even take advantage of last-minute clearance sections to grab items I need before Christmas. My grandkids always love helping me find presents during these sales – we have fun browsing together and making a list of must-haves. It's become quite the event!

Portion control is quite simple for me. You know, my late husband always said, "Waste not, want not." So, we were taught to make sure we didn't throw away food because we couldn't finish a meal or two. We'd save leftovers for later, freeze them, or share with our children. It's still one of those habits I try to carry forward. Now, as an older person living alone, I find myself being more mindful of what I eat, especially since I have some health issues like arthritis in my hands and joints. A smaller serving size helps reduce strain while cooking and eating, too. Plus, it feels less overwhelming trying to manage everything all at once – and it makes me feel better knowing I'm taking care of myself properly.

I think I'd find a gentle, empathetic approach helpful. You know, someone who understands my perspective, even when we might differ. A therapist who encourages open discussion and helps me unpack the reasons behind my thoughts and feelings, rather than just offering solutions. It's funny, isn't it, how sometimes all we need is someone to listen and validate our concerns before we can start moving forward? My late husband used to say I was very rational, but he knew better – there were plenty of moments when I needed someone to just sit quietly beside me and let me work out what I wanted to do myself.

I think prevention is key, really. For me, starting my day a bit later than some people does mean I have more control over how busy I am. Getting up at 6:30 AM gives me enough time to meditate, have a cuppa, and plan out what I need to tackle before diving into work. Of course, everyone's different, so I'm sure there must be other strategies people find helpful. As someone who's been juggling various jobs and responsibilities over many decades, I've found taking breaks throughout the day helps – even just stepping outside for fresh air or chatting with a neighbour. And I try not to put too much pressure on myself; setting achievable targets rather than trying to cram everything into one go makes all the difference. It sounds simple, perhaps, but sometimes that's exactly what you need!

I think a big part of my success lies in establishing a bedtime routine, you know. For me, winding down before bed is essential – I try to relax with a cuppa tea, maybe some gentle stretching or listening to calming music. And I always make sure my room is dark, quiet, and cool; it helps signal to my brain that it's time to shut down. After getting ready for bed, I like to spend a bit of time writing in my journal – jotting down thoughts, gratitudes, and plans for the next day. It really helps clear my mind and sets intentions. Plus, it gives me something tangible to reflect on later. As for alarm clocks, I'm rather strict about using mine – set to ring between 6:25 and 6:35 am, so I have plenty of time to wake up feeling refreshed. Of course, having a glass of water beside my bedside table helps too! A simple habit, perhaps, but one that works wonders for staying hydrated throughout the day.

I like to mix it up a bit to avoid boredom. You know, some days I'll go for a brisk walk along our local park path, while others I might pop into my backyard to tend to my vegetable patch – digging, weeding, watering... all that sort of thing. If the weather permits, I love taking a stroll around the nearby woods; there's something so peaceful about being surrounded by nature. And of course, I always make sure to squeeze in some stretching exercises whenever I'm watching the telly or listening to podcasts during the day. Can't be too keen on sitting still for long! On Sundays, I usually try to fit in some yoga poses before bed to unwind. Just a gentle practice to clear me mind and prepare for another busy week ahead. Simple pleasures, really!

I like to think it out thoroughly before making a decision. You know, weighing all the pros and cons, considering my priorities and what will work best for me. It's almost like sifting through seeds while gardening – sometimes you have to weed out what isn't going to be successful so you can nurture what is. When faced with choices, I ask myself questions like "What are the benefits?" and "Are there any drawbacks?" And really take my time to think them through.

I think feedback is a vital part of any process, whether personal or professional. When receiving feedback, I try to listen attentively and reflect on what was said, making sure I fully grasp the point being made. It helps me identify areas where I might need improvement and adjust my approach accordingly. For instance, during my work days, I'd always ask colleagues and supervisors for constructive comments, which helped me refine my skills and adapt to changing requirements. Now, in retirement, my grandkids occasionally offer helpful insights – it warms my heart knowing they're paying attention! My response is usually to thank them kindly and consider their suggestions thoughtfully before deciding how best to proceed. However, sometimes people find me a bit too particular, so I'm aware of needing to balance listening actively while keeping an open mind. By incorporating diverse viewpoints, I aim to develop more comprehensive and practical solutions rather than simply accepting one perspective. As I say, "patience and curiosity" have gotten me far in life; similarly, embracing different opinions enriches decision-making processes.

I think my approach is quite straightforward really. When faced with a problem or puzzle, I like to take a step back and try to understand why things work the way they do. What underlying principles or assumptions am I applying here? Sometimes it takes me a while to break everything down into its component parts, but once I have all those bits sorted out, it becomes much clearer what needs to be done. It's almost like unwrapping an onion – each layer gets revealed gradually! And of course, there's always the possibility that someone else might know something better, so I'm happy to listen and consider alternative views too.

I find that taking a moment to slow down and reflect helps me clarify my thoughts. Sometimes, writing them down in a journal or talking to myself out loud really brings everything into focus. And of course, discussing things with trusted people like my grandkids or close friends does too. They often have different perspectives which can be really helpful in putting things into perspective. But I think what works best is actually making sure I ask plenty of questions – why something happened, how it affects others, all those sorts of things. It's funny, isn't it? As an older person, one tends to appreciate simplicity more and a willingness to listen rather than trying to rush towards answers straight away.

I think I'm always looking out for opportunities to improve myself, whether that's trying a new hobby or activity, taking an online course, or simply being more mindful of my habits. For me, it's really about staying curious and open to new ideas – even if they might be outside of my comfort zone. Sometimes it'll be something like watching a documentary or listening to a podcast on a topic I know nothing about, which will spark some interesting insights. Other times it'll be asking someone younger who knows better what they're doing, or taking advantage of free resources available locally, such as workshops or classes at the community centre. It's funny how sometimes we only notice our limitations until we try something else; so identifying those areas where we need improvement is all about paying attention to any gaps in knowledge or skills and finding ways to bridge them. And it's okay to make mistakes along the way – I believe that's part of growing! What's key is keeping a bit of humility and acknowledging that there's still plenty we could learn.

I think my approach is quite simple, really. It's all about being patient and curious. You know, people often rush into things without taking a moment to consider whether it'll be a good fit. But I find that slowing down and asking yourself why something might work allows you to make more informed decisions. Plus, there's always room to learn and adapt – even at an older age! My motto has become "patience and curiosity" – they seem to serve me pretty well so far.

I make sure to prioritize my reading time, especially since my husband was away a lot while we were married, so I had a bit more free time to myself. Now, even though he's been gone for six years now, I still manage to squeeze in some reading whenever I can. My morning is usually quite busy, getting breakfast ready, having a cuppa...but once I'm all settled, I'll sit down with a book and be lost for a couple of hours before the rest of the day gets too hectic. To be honest, most of what I read these days are historical novels or biographies – there's something about immersing oneself in someone else's story that really draws me in. And I must admit, sometimes I sneak into other genres like mystery or detective fiction when I need a break from reality! It's amazing how much escapism books provide, isn't it? If I'm being completely truthful, though, finding quiet time has become increasingly difficult lately. The grandkids come over frequently, which always brings its own brand of joy, but also adds an extra layer of chaos to our lives. Still, I wouldn't trade those moments for the world – watching them grow, seeing the way they interact with each other, makes every effort worth

I think I discovered my love for historical fiction and biography quite unintentionally, really. As a child growing up, my mother was an avid reader and would always have books lying around the house. One day, while rummaging through her shelves, I stumbled upon Agatha Christie – who became one of my all-time favourite authors! Her clever mysteries kept me hooked, and I devoured every single one of them. After getting married, my husband and I moved into our first little flat together. Our local library had an amazing selection of books, which sparked another passion for me – particularly historical biographies. We'd spend many evenings listening to audiobooks during our Sunday roasts; Barbara Tuchman and Elizabeth Longford were some of the ones we enjoyed. Their engaging storytelling drew us in and made history come alive! Nowadays, word-of-mouth recommendations play a big part in introducing me to fresh voices and styles. Friends will bring along novels they think I'll appreciate, like Philippa Gregory or Kate Morton, based on what we discuss afterwards. Occasionally, I stumble upon articles or interviews in magazines or newspapers where writers talk about the craft and share insights into the writing process. This helps me better comprehend why certain elements make something resonate so deeply. Sometimes, browsing through

I love listening to music! When I'm getting ready for a day out, like going to visit my grandkids, I find it helps me set the right atmosphere. I put on some upbeat tunes – nothing too loud, mind you – and tidy up a bit, make sure everything is shipshape before heading out. Sometimes, if we're having dinner together, I might even sing along softly. My husband used to tease me about how much I loved singing in the kitchen; now, I think it brings a smile to everyone's face! It's become a little ritual really.

I'm a big fan of listening while I work or during my daily commute, actually. It helps me focus and makes the most of my morning productivity session before my usual slump in the afternoon. As an avid reader, I have to say I find having someone narrate a story really brings out characters and descriptions much better than reading them myself. Audiobooks are perfect for catching up on history lessons, memoirs, or getting lost in some thrilling mysteries – especially since my eyes aren't what they used to be! But sometimes I still like switching between reading actual books and listening; there's something special about turning pages and experiencing those tactile moments.

I find joy in keeping my hands busy with various hobbies. Gardening is a big one – there's something therapeutic about getting out into nature and watching plants grow. My little vegetable patch is quite rewarding, especially during harvest season. It brings me peace, and I love sharing some of those fresh vegetables with my grandkids. In addition to gardening, I'm an avid reader, particularly enjoying history books and biographies. There's nothing like curling up with a good story or learning more about someone's life. I'd say I spend several hours each week lost in a novel or absorbing information on different subjects. Occasionally, I'll attend local library events or join in on community gatherings where we discuss literature or other topics of interest. Those interactions always make the activity feel less solitary and more enjoyable. And let's not forget baking! Preparing meals and treats for my loved ones never fails to bring smiles onto everyone's faces. Sometimes, I even experiment with new recipes while listening to music or podcasts, which adds another layer of pleasure to the process. Overall, staying active has become all about finding balance between relaxation and engagement – I believe these simple pleasures have helped me cope better with the demands of daily life.

I find my best moments are spent enjoying some peace and quiet, surrounded by nature. Whether it's tending to my vegetable garden, taking a leisurely walk along our quiet streets, or curled up with a good book, being alone allows me to recharge and reflect. It gives me space to think, clear out any cluttered thoughts, and simply be myself. There's something therapeutic about having the freedom to set my own pace and indulge in whatever brings me joy – whether that's baking, writing, or even watching an old favourite film. Time alone isn't always easy, especially now with grandkids growing older and less frequently visiting, but it remains essential to my sense of balance and contentment.

When trying a new cuisine, I like to start by getting recommendations from people I trust, such as my grandkids or friends who have visited places where the food originated. They usually give me some tips on what dishes are must-tries and which ones to avoid. Then, I'll read reviews and cookbooks to get an idea of what to expect. I'm quite traditional in my cooking habits, so sometimes I might stick close to classic recipes, but I love experimenting with different flavors and ingredients to try and recreate authentic dishes at home. My late husband used to say I was very stubborn once we'd found something we liked – he said I couldn't bear to deviate from our tried-and-tested favorites! It's all about balance between being adventurous and enjoying comfort foods, isn't it? A bit too exotic or unfamiliar can be overwhelming, but there's nothing quite like discovering a new favorite dish to make you feel excited and eager to share it with others. Now, I find myself looking forward to Sunday lunches out at local restaurants exploring other cultures while still keeping room for homemade classics like roast chicken and Yorkshire puddings!

I like to mix things up between more structured activities and some quieter ones. For instance, my weekend mornings might be spent tending to the garden – there's something therapeutic about getting out among the plants and watching them grow. Then later, we'll have a family gathering or visit our grandkids, which is always lively and full of laughter. And in between, I try to squeeze in some reading or perhaps a spot of baking – I find those little moments really enjoyable. It helps me feel fulfilled and refreshed before the afternoon slump sets in. On weekdays, my evenings usually start winding down with a bit of television or listening to radio, maybe catching up on current events. If I'm feeling particularly inspired, I might whip up one of my famous scones while keeping an ear tuned into some history docos or nature programmes. All these different aspects of my life allow me to avoid monotony and make sure every day has its unique rhythm.

I have a little notebook where I jot down my daily plans, including any fun activities like going out for tea with the girls or playing bridge with my grandkids. It helps me stay organised and ensures I don't forget what we're supposed to be doing today. Occasionally, I'll make notes on gardening progress too – keeping track of which seeds need planting and harvesting. My daughter likes coming into our sitting room and looking at them; she says it's her favourite thing to do before bedtime!

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned person, I suppose! *laughs* But seriously, I find that having routines helps me stay organised. Every morning, before my husband sadly passed away, we'd sit together and plan out our daily schedules, including mine, so I knew exactly what needed to be done each day. Since he was gone, my granddaughter has been very helpful, checking in regularly to make sure everything is running smoothly here. In terms of actual organisation, I have a calendar where I jot down all my appointments, doctor's visits, and any other regular tasks like grocery shopping or tidying the house. It's quite simple, really – nothing too fancy. And, of course, I always try to put things into perspective; remember, I'm 70 now, and some things aren't worth stressing about! My secretaries work lives were probably more hectic than my current relaxed pace, but even back then, I found value in making lists, prioritising tasks, and breaking them down into manageable chunks. The key, in my opinion, is finding what works for you and sticking to it. Now, I might rely less on paper calendars and more on reminders on my tablet – though sometimes those still drive me a tad mad because I struggle keeping track of

I start my morning with a nice cuppa tea, sit quietly for a bit to gather my thoughts while enjoying the sunshine streaming into the room. After that, I usually spend some time checking emails, responding to any urgent messages, and perhaps making a mental note of what I need to tackle during the day. Sometimes I'll glance over my calendar to check if there are any upcoming appointments or meetings I should be aware of. It really helps me feel more centered and prepared for whatever comes ahead.

I think my main focus has been trying to reduce heat loss where possible, especially since I live in a rather draughty old terraced house. So, I make sure all windows are closed during cold snaps, put draught excluders round doors, and have added some insulation to loft space recently. We've also got an older boiler which isn't exactly fuel-efficient, so we're looking into replacing it soon. My son does try to be mindful too; he knows how much heating costs me. I'm not keen on spending money unnecessarily, though! Still, every little bit helps, and I suppose using thick curtains or blinds when needed makes a difference. Simple measures like these really add up in the end.

I must admit, I'm still getting used to all these smart devices, but my grandkids have helped me set them up. They think it's quite clever having a voice assistant like Alexa to control everything. However, I find myself avoiding using it most of the time because, between you and me, I prefer simple solutions. Still, it does make some things easier, especially when my memory isn't what it used to be – like remembering to turn lights on/off or adjusting the thermostat. To be honest, I struggle with updating software and figuring out how certain features work. My son helps occasionally, so I appreciate his guidance. But overall, I'd say our smart home setup is more functional than exciting; we mainly stick to the basics and try not to complicate things too much.

I think prioritising is quite simple, really. You have to weigh what's most important against what can wait a bit longer. For instance, my grandkids will always come first – making sure they're happy and growing strong takes precedence. After that, taking care of myself and keeping the garden tidy comes into play. If there's something specific that needs fixing or maintenance, like the washing machine, I'll make space for that too, but only if it affects daily routines significantly. It's all about finding balance, isn't it? Sometimes having some flexibility helps – being able to adapt and adjust priorities based on changing circumstances makes a big difference.
